title: Kohn-Luttinger Superconductivity of Weyl Fermi Arcs in PtBi$_2$
authors: Reuel Dsouza, Nikolaos Parthenios, Brian M. Andersen, Morten H. Christensen
abstract: Recent experimental observations in the noncentrosymmetric Weyl semimetal PtBi$_2$ indicate unconventional superconductivity hosted by topological surface states -- Weyl Fermi arcs -- with a node at the center of each arc. Focusing on these Fermi arcs, we calculate the electronically mediated pairing interaction using a Kohn-Luttinger approach and find that, in a large region of the phase diagram, the leading superconducting instability has an $i$-wave symmetry featuring precisely such an intra-arc node. We study the dependence of the leading superconducting instabilities on electronic interaction parameters and chemical potential and show that the $i$-wave state is robust to changes in the model parameters. Our results provide a possible mechanism for the observation of topological $i$-wave superconductivity on the surface of PtBi$_2$ and may have implications for the broader landscape of superconducting instabilities arising from repulsive interactions on the surfaces of Weyl semimetals.
summary: This theoretical work calculates the superconducting instability in the Weyl semimetal PtBi2 using the Kohn-Luttinger mechanism. By analyzing the pairing interaction on the topological surface Fermi arcs, the authors predict that the superconducting state is most likely an i-wave symmetry. This provides a microscopic mechanism for observing topological superconductivity in such materials.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature and symmetry of unconventional superconductivity arising from electron-mediated pairing interactions in Weyl semimetals.
main_result: The Kohn-Luttinger approach predicts a dominant nodal i-wave superconducting state in a large region of the phase diagram for PtBi2, suggesting a mechanism for topological superconductivity on the surface.
method: The authors use the Kohn-Luttinger approach, calculating the effective pairing interaction by dressing the bare Coulomb repulsion and solving the linearized gap equation projected onto the Weyl Fermi arcs.
model_or_system: The physical system is the noncentrosymmetric Weyl semimetal PtBi2. The model employs a phenomenological patch model derived from the surface Weyl Fermi arcs, built upon a tight-binding Hamiltonian describing the bulk electronic structure.
key_observables: Superconducting order parameter symmetry (i-wave, s-wave), the superconducting gap structure, and the phase diagram dependence on interaction parameters.
important_parameters: Bare repulsive interaction strength (U), chemical potential ($\mu$), and various hopping/coupling parameters ($\alpha, \gamma$).
main_assumptions: The superconductivity instability is assumed to be driven purely by Coulomb repulsion, and the model simplifies the complex surface physics into a patch approximation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the phase diagram showing regions for different superconducting symmetries (s-wave, i-wave) and schematic representations of the electronic interactions governing the pairing.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the electronic structure of the Weyl semimetal using a tight-binding model, then applies the Kohn-Luttinger formalism to derive the effective pairing interaction, culminating in a phase diagram analysis of the superconducting symmetry.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter superconductivity, the use of symmetry analysis (noncentrosymmetry, topological features) and pairing mechanisms driven by repulsive interactions connects to general many-body physics concepts relevant to open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phenomenological model. (a) Phase diagram of the 12-patch model assuming g0◦> 0. To visualize the parameter space we additionally set w1 60◦= w2 60◦. The re- gion marked ’No solution’ is characterized by the absence of a positive eigenvalue in the 12-patch model. (b) Electronic interactions gθ and wθ of the 12-patch model. These corre- spond to intra- and inter-chirality scattering processes with fermionic indistinguishability taken into account.

FIG. 2. Electronic structure of minimal model. (a) Top view of the bulk Brillouin zone (BZ) with 12 Weyl points. Cir- cles/crosses denote Weyl points above/below the kz = 0 plane with blue being positive chirality and red negative chirality. (b) Fermi arcs at E = 0 on the surface BZ with the projected Weyl points colored green and brown. (c) Band structure for the slab geometry along the Γ −M −K −Γ path.

FIG. 4. Kohn-Luttinger phase diagram and supercon- ducting order parameters. (a) Symmetry of the supercon- ducting order parameter as a function of U, J/U and µ, the chemical potential relative to the Weyl point. Near the Weyl point, the i-wave state is dominant, while the nodal s-wave state becomes increasingly favored as the Fermi level moves away from the Weyl point. Superconducting order parame- ters appearing in the phase diagram: (b) s-wave, (c) nodal s-wave, and (d) i-wave.
